channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] A Dab of DuckDB
item.description:
The post shows DuckDB as a local analytical workbench that can query JSON, CSV, TSV, and Parquet with SQL, then gives a concrete 22,229-file JSON import example. The useful part is the boundary setting: DuckDB fits read-heavy single-node analytics on local SSDs, handles datasets larger than RAM, and can move from exploratory imports to file-backed databases and Parquet for repeat work.

  • DuckDB can turn a directory of semi-structured files into a queryable SQL table with little setup.
  • The best fit is local analytical work where the dataset fits on one machine and random writes are limited.
  • Resource pragmas let you cap memory and CPU when running DuckDB inside normal developer workflows.
  • Converting raw JSON to Parquet or using file-backed databases changes performance characteristics for repeated analysis.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] How the Lobsters front page works
item.description:
The article reverse-engineers Lobsters ranking into base tag modifiers, logarithmic vote and comment-point terms, a fixed age term, and gates that limit controversial low-score posts. The algorithm section is concrete, and the closing reflection is useful because it separates ranking mechanics from the moderation, invite, and participation norms that shape the community.

  • Tag modifiers and self-authored status set the initial rank before engagement arrives.
  • Votes and eligible comment points grow logarithmically, so early engagement matters more than late volume.
  • Age grows linearly and eventually dominates, which prevents old posts from staying on the front page indefinitely.
  • Community quality comes from moderation and participation incentives as much as ranking math.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] My audio interface has ssh enabled by default
item.description:
The author captures a Rodecaster Duo firmware update, finds a gzipped tarball without signature enforcement, observes default SSH public keys, and reconstructs the HID update flow with two simple commands. It is a compact hardware-security case study with a practical AI-assisted debugging angle: packet capture plus Claude Code was enough to map the update protocol and build custom firmware.

  • Firmware-update paths need signature checks, recovery design, and explicit remote-access posture.
  • Default SSH keys on embedded devices create unclear ownership and disclosure risk even when password login is disabled.
  • Capturing disk and USB traffic can expose enough protocol structure to reproduce an updater manually.
  • AI assistance can accelerate reverse-engineering chores when the human validates packet boundaries and device behavior.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] On sabotaging projects by overthinking, scope creep, and structural diffing
item.description:
The essay uses woodworking and software-tool experiments to show how searching for prior art can silently expand a small project into a vague research program. The durable lesson is to define success criteria early, especially for agent-built tooling, because exploration around structural diffing, fuzzy search, or LLM workflows can consume the time that should have produced the minimal useful result.

  • Clear success criteria keep project scope tied to the original problem.
  • Prior-art research is valuable when it informs a decision, and expensive when it keeps redefining the target.
  • Tool-building projects need a deliberate stop condition before abstraction work begins.
  • Small finished utilities can teach more than large design spaces left unresolved.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Modern LZ Compression Part 2: FSE and Arithmetic Coding
item.description:
The article takes a working LZ plus Huffman compressor and moves it toward modern practice with Silesia benchmarks, arithmetic-coding background, ANS and FSE explanation, FSE-coded sequence streams, repeat offsets, parser changes, and literal-table cleanup. The strongest idea is that compression gains often come from designing a better symbol stream before tuning the entropy coder.

  • A wider benchmark set exposes whether compression changes generalize beyond one friendly corpus.
  • FSE and ANS give arithmetic-coding-like compression with implementation tradeoffs suited to modern codecs.
  • Repeat offsets and sequence-aware parsing improve the model the entropy coder sees.
  • Table representation and symbol-stream structure can matter as much as the coding algorithm itself.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] The Contract Your Test Did Not Mean to Sign
item.description:
The post argues that tests encode contracts at a specific scope, and brittle e2e suites often fail because the encoded contract moves faster than the product promise. The practical lens is to choose the surface deliberately: UI-scope tests protect UI behavior, application-scope tests protect capabilities, and lower-level checks should absorb mechanical churn where possible.

  • Readable test code can still bind a suite to unstable interface mechanics.
  • The first design question for a test is the promise it protects and the conditions that should make it change.
  • Application-scope helpers can protect capabilities across UI, API, MCP, or other entry points.
  • Clear contract scope creates faster feedback loops for humans and coding agents.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Clock Synchronization Is a Nightmare
item.description:
The article surveys clock drift, skew, and ordering failures across builds, databases, tracing, NTP, PTP, logical clocks, vector clocks, and Spanner TrueTime. The useful model is bounded uncertainty: physical clocks can support distributed guarantees only when the system carries explicit uncertainty or shifts ordering to causality-aware logical time.

  • Clock skew breaks build systems, databases, and traces by making effects appear before causes.
  • NTP works for millisecond-class synchronization, while PTP needs hardware timestamping and network support for sub-microsecond precision.
  • Logical and vector clocks model causality without depending on one global physical timestamp.
  • TrueTime-style APIs expose an uncertainty interval, which lets systems wait or reason before committing ordering-sensitive work.
